coredata.dc:title: Pseudomonas aeruginosa-derived carbon dots doped with sulfur as active packaging materials for fresh food preservation
coredata.dc:creator: Hong, Su Jung
Riahi, Zohreh
Shin, Gye Hwa
Kim, Jun Tae
coredata.dc:description: Bacteria (Pseudomonas aeruginosa)-derived carbon dots (PCDs) were successfully synthesized via a hydrothermal method and further doped with sulfur to improve their functional properties, such as antioxidant and antimicrobial activities. Sulfur-doped PCDs (S-PCDs) exhibited excellent antioxidant activities of 94.98% for ABTS and 83.63% for DPPH radical scavenging rate and strong antimicrobial activity of 21.63 mm inhibition zone against Listeria monocytogenes with a slight reduction in the photoluminescence intensity. S-PCDs were incorporated into polyvinyl alcohol (PVA)-based films with different concentrations (0.5, 1, and 2%) and significantly increased the UV-blocking property from 22.55 to 97.17% without affecting the film transparency. When the PVA/S-PCDs composite films were used to package pork meat and sliced apples, the shelf life of these foods can be extended by reducing microbial contamination of pork meat and inhibiting the browning of sliced apples. Therefore, PVA/S-PCDs composite films showed great potential as an active packaging for various foods.

head.ce:title: Pseudomonas aeruginosa-derived carbon dots doped with sulfur as active packaging materials for fresh food preservation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Bacteria (Pseudomonas aeruginosa)-derived carbon dots (PCDs) were successfully synthesized via a hydrothermal method and further doped with sulfur to improve their functional properties, such as antioxidant and antimicrobial activities. Sulfur-doped PCDs (S-PCDs) exhibited excellent antioxidant activities of 94.98% for ABTS and 83.63% for DPPH radical scavenging rate and strong antimicrobial activity of 21.63 mm inhibition zone against Listeria monocytogenes with a slight reduction in the photoluminescence intensity. S-PCDs were incorporated into polyvinyl alcohol (PVA)-based films with different concentrations (0.5, 1, and 2%) and significantly increased the UV-blocking property from 22.55 to 97.17% without affecting the film transparency. When the PVA/S-PCDs composite films were used to package pork meat and sliced apples, the shelf life of these foods can be extended by reducing microbial contamination of pork meat and inhibiting the browning of sliced apples. Therefore, PVA/S-PCDs composite films showed great potential as an active packaging for various foods.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Food waste is an important and urgent global problem with negative environmental, social, and economic impacts. According to the Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations (FAO), about one-third of all food produced globally is lost or wasted, amounting to about $1 trillion per year (Xue et al., 2017). Besides the economic losses, food waste contributes to greenhouse gas emissions (Mahmudul, Akbar, Rasul, Narayanan, & Mofijur, 2022), exacerbates resource depletion (Foley et al., 2011), and perpetuates food insecurity and poverty (Munesue, Masui, & Fushima, 2015). In recent years, the food waste problem has received increasing attention and many countries and organizations have developed strategies and initiatives to address it. Despite these efforts, food waste remains a major problem and requires further research and action to reduce its impact.

Lack of proper food packaging technology is considered one of the major contributing factors to significant consumer-level food waste in developed countries (Aschemann-Witzel, De Hooge, Amani, Bech-Larsen, & Oostindjer, 2015). Food packaging plays an important role in protecting food from physical, chemical, and biological damage ensuring its safety and quality throughout its shelf life. Active packaging is a powerful solution to the food waste problem by improving the functionality of the packaging. Active packaging refers to packaging systems that actively interact with food by removing or releasing substances to maintain or improve quality and shelf life (Paritosh et al., 2017). By incorporating active substances into packaging, such as antimicrobials (Satriaji, Garcia, Kim, Shin, & Kim, 2020), oxygen scavengers (Demirhan & Candoĝan, 2017), or moisture absorbers (Gaikwad, Singh, & Ajji, 2019), active packaging can extend the shelf life of foods by creating an unfavorable environment for microbial growth or oxidative deterioration.

Recently numerous studies have been conducted on active packaging to extend the shelf life of fruits, vegetables, and meat products. Wei, Li, Zhang, Seidi, and Xiao (2023) introduced ethylene-scavenging active packaging utilizing dendritic mesoporous silica-supported platinum (Pt/DMS) and demonstrated its potential to preserve fruits and vegetables, and notably extend the post-harvest shelf life of bananas. In the study by Xie, Pan, and Cai (2022), bagasse cellulose/polyvinyl alcohol composite films were developed through hydroxyl cross-linking. Testing the preservation capacity of this composite film on cherries revealed its effectiveness in preserving food along with its excellent biodegradability when exposed to soil. Pan, Xie, and Cai (2022), used cellulose derived from sugarcane as a matrix and PVA and ZnO as performance enhancers. Preservation tests on red grapes demonstrated that the cross-linked composite films were effective in preserving food while remaining biodegradable nature when exposed to soil conditions.

Active packaging films are manufactured using various polymer matrices that deliver and support these active fillers. Recently, the packaging industry and scientists have emphasized the use of biopolymers such as cellulose and chitosan instead of petroleum-based packaging to reduce the environmental impact of plastic packaging (Min, Ezati, Yoon, & Rhim, 2023; Saedi, Kim, Lee, Kumar, & Shin, 2023). Poly (vinyl alcohol) (PVA) is an eco-friendly bioplastic that is attracting attention due to its excellent chemical resistance, film-forming properties, non-toxicity, biocompatibility, and economic feasibility (Oun, Shin, Rhim, & Kim, 2022). It is a semi-crystalline polymer synthesized by hydrolysis of poly (vinyl acetate). PVA and PVA-based composites are used in a variety of applications, such as paper coating, packaging, optical films, stabilizers, pharmaceutical caches, and drug delivery (Abdullah & Dong, 2019). However, poor mechanical and water vapor barrier properties and lack of functional properties limit its use in the food packaging industry. The physical and functional properties of PVA-based films can be improved by adding appropriate fillers. Incorporating nanomaterials such as carbon dots (CDs) and metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) into biopolymers-based active packaging has emerged as a promising approach to improve the functionality and performance of packaging materials (Khan, Ezati, Kim, & Rhim, 2022; Riahi, Hong, Rhim, Shin, & Kim, 2023a, b).

CDs are zero-dimensional nanomaterials with unique optical and electronic properties that make them attractive for a wide range of applications (Min, Ezati, & Rhim, 2022). It has a size range of 1–10 nm and is composed of carbon atoms arranged in a graphene-like structure (Ezati, Khan, Rhim, Kim, & Molaei, 2023). CDs have gained significant attention for various biological applications due to their favorable properties such as good biocompatibility, photoluminescence, and low toxicity. Recent studies have highlighted the excellent antioxidant and antibacterial characteristics of CDs and applied as active packaging materials. For instance, Khan, Ezati, and Rhim (2023) reported that the chitosan/starch/CD composite films exhibited excellent antioxidant properties and antibacterial effects against Escherichia coli, Listeria monocytogenes, and Staphylococcus aureus. Similarly, Zhao, Zhang, Mujumdar, Adhikari, and Wang (2022) demonstrated that CD-incorporated PVA films exhibited excellent UV-blocking, antioxidant, and antibacterial properties resulting in the extended shelf life of bananas, jujubes, and fried meatballs, respectively. These findings highlight the potential of CDs in developing innovative active packaging systems that can address the challenges of food contamination and spoilage. Various carbon sources, including carbohydrates (e.g., glucose, fructose), natural resources (e.g., coal, graphite), and organic waste materials (e.g., fruit and vegetable peel), have been used to synthesize CDs. However, few studies have been reported on the characterization of CDs synthesized from microorganism sources. Microorganisms can be used as concentrated sources of carbon (Romanova & Sazhin, 2010). Furthermore, utilizing CDs is a sustainable, cost-effective, and eco-friendly approach that is unaffected by seasonal, spatial, and temporal changes (Hua, Bao, Wang, Chen, & Wu, 2017).

Therefore, this study aimed to synthesize Pseudomonas aeruginosa-derived carbon dots (PCDs) as a practical filler and enhance their functional characteristics by doping with sulfur (S-PCDs). The characteristics of PCDs and S-PCDs were compared by measuring their PL intensity, antioxidant activity, and antibacterial activity. In addition, multifunctional PVA-based composite films were developed by incorporating S-PCDs for active packaging applications. The prepared composite films were characterized by their surface color, UV-light barrier, mechanical strength, water vapor barrier, and antioxidant activity. The pork meat and sliced apples were wrapped with the fabricated films to evaluate their effectiveness in inhibiting bacterial growth and browning, respectively. To our knowledge, this is the first study to fabricate PVA-based films by incorporating sulfur-doped P. aeruginosa-derived CDs (S-PCDs) and applied to real food products (pork meat and sliced apples).

PCDs and S-PCDs were successfully synthesized via a hydrothermal method using P. aeruginosa cells and doping with sulfur as shown in Fig. 1 . Briefly, P. aeruginosa was cultured in Brain Heart Infusion (BHI) medium in a shaking incubator (JSSI-100C, JS Research Inc., Gongju, Korea) at 37 °C until the bacterial cells growth up to 7 × 108 cells/mL. Then, the culture medium was removed by centrifugation at 4146×g at 4 °C for 15 min. The collected P. aeruginosa cells were washed 3 times and re-suspended in 50 mL of DW. For sulfur doping, 0.3 g of sulfur was dispersed into the above solution in a polytetrafluoroethylene (Teflon) lined stainless steel reactor and reacted at 200 °C in a muffle furnace (Daihan FX-27, Daihan Scientific, Wonju, Korea) for 6 h. After cooling, the supernatant was filtered using a 0.22 μm membrane filter to remove impurities. The PCDs were also prepared using the same procedure without sulfur.

Poly (vinyl alcohol) (PVA, 9.5 g) and glycerol (4.75 g) were added to 190 mL of distilled water with magnetic stirring at 95 °C for 2 h. Subsequently, S-PCDs (0, 0.5, 1, and 2 wt% based on PVA) were added to the above solution and mixed at room temperature (25 ± 2 °C) with 200 rpm for 20 min, and the prepared film solution was degassed by stirring at 80 rpm for 2 h. The prepared film solution was cast on a Teflon-coated glass plate and dried at room temperature for 48 h. After 48 h, the films were peeled off from the plate and conditioned at 25 °C and 50% relative humidity (RH) for 48 h before testing their properties. The prepared composite ﬁlms were designated as neat PVA, PVA/S-PCDs0.5%, PVA/S-PCDs1%, and PVA/S-PCDs2% depending on the amount of S-PCD added.

UV–Vis absorption spectra were measured using an Optizen POP Series UV/Vis spectrophotometer with a 1 cm light path cuvette (Mecasys Co. Ltd., Daejeon, Korea). Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy experiment was performed by attenuated total reflectance in the range 4000 to 650 cm−1 using a JASCO Spectroscopy FT-IR 4× (JASCO Co., Tokyo, Japan). Fluorescence measurements of P. aeruginosa, PCDs, and S-PCDs were performed using a 1 cm square quartz cuvette on a spectrofluorophotometer (RF-6000, Shimadzu Corp., Kyoto, Japan). Emission intensities were measured for excitation wavelengths (300–450 nm) for intensity comparison. Emission scans were performed with an excitation slit of 5 nm, an emission slit of 5 nm, and a scan rate of 1200 nm/min. Morphological analysis was performed using a field emission transmission electron microscope (FE-TEM, JEM-2100F, Jeol Ltd., Tokyo, Japan) to determine the size and shape of the synthesized S-PCDs.

L929 fibroblasts were employed for assessing the biocompatibility of S-PCDs. To conduct this evaluation, L929 cells were cultured in Dulbecco's modified Eagle medium (DMEM) from Corning (Glendale, AZ, USA). The medium was supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (Gibco, Franklin Lakes, NJ, USA) and 1% antibiotic/antimycotic solution. The cell culture took place in a humidified incubator with 5% CO2 at 37 °C. Media refreshment occurred every two days until 80% cell confluence was attained. The 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyl-tetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay, which a method for indirectly measuring cell viability, was employed. This assay relies on the reaction of mitochondrial dehydrogenase to convert tetrazolium salts into insoluble formazan (a purple compound) in metabolically active cells. In the MTT test, L929 cells (7 × 103 cells/well) were seeded in 96-well plates and left to incubate overnight. Subsequently, cells were subjected to various concentrations of S-PCDs (0.1, 0.2, and 0.5 mg/mL) for 24–72 h. Following treatment, 20 μL of a 2 mg/mL MTT solution was added to each well and allowed to incubate at 37 °C for 3h. After dissolving formazan crystals with dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), absorbance at 570 nm was monitored using a microplate reader from Molecular Devices (San Jose, CA, USA). The presented data represent the mean ± standard deviation (SD) derived from a minimum of three independent experiments, each performed in triplicate.

Radical scavenging rates of ABTS (2,2-Azino-bis(3- 3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) and DPPH (2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl) were determined to measure the antioxidant activities of PCDs, S-PCDs, and the composites film samples according to Oun, Shin, and Kim (2022). ABTS was dissolved in a 2.45 mM of potassium persulfate solution to achieve a concentration of 7 mM, and then the mixture was incubated overnight in the dark to generate free radicals. After that, it was diluted with distilled water to prepare an ABTS solution such that the absorbance at 734 nm was 0.8 ± 0.02. In addition, 0.2 mM of DPPH was dissolved in methanol to prepare a DPPH solution.

To measure the antioxidant activity of carbon dots, ABTS and DPPH solutions prepared in a 96-microwell plate were reacted with various concentrations of PCDs and S-PCDs (50–1000 μg/mL). Plates were incubated in the dark prior to measuring absorbance at 734 nm for ABTS and 517 nm for DPPH using a multi-detection microplate reader.

To measure the antioxidant activity of the composite films, 50 mg of each film sample was mixed with the 10 mL of ABTS or DPPH solutions and incubated at a dark place for 2 h. The absorbance was then measured using a UV–visible spectrophotometer at 734 nm for ABTS and 517 nm for DPPH.

Agar well diffusion assay (Balouiri, Sadiki, & Ibnsouda, 2016; Riahi et al., 2023a, b) was performed to evaluate the antibacterial activity against Listeria monocytogenes. The surface of the agar plate was inoculated by spreading the bacteria in a population of 105 CFU/mL. Then, a 5.5 mm diameter hole was punched on the surface of the agar plate and 100 μL of PCDs or S-PCDs solutions were added to the well. Plates were incubated at 37 °C for 12 h, and the formation of inhibition zones in the plates was observed.

The release test of S-PCDs released from the PVA-based composite films was performed using food stimulants such as water, 50% ethanol, and 4% acetic acid. The film specimens (2.5 cm × 2.5 cm) were immersed in 20 mL of food simulants and maintained at 25 °C with moderate shaking. The solution absorbance was then measured at 290 nm at specified intervals of 1 h using a UV–vis spectrophotometer.

Atomic force microscopy (AFM, Park FX-40, Park Systems Corp., Suwon, Korea) was performed to investigate the surface roughness of PVA-based films in non-contact mode. The films were conditioned at 25 °C and 50% RH prior to the AFM analysis. Film samples (1.0 cm × 1.0 cm) were fixed in a sample holder using double-sided adhesive tape, and four randomly chosen areas (10 μm2) were evaluated. Tapping mode was programmed with an amplitude of 20.47 nm, set point of 9.78 nm, a frequency of 0.7 Hz, and 2 s scan per 256 pixel line. This process gave a 256 × 256 data matrix, where each pixel represents a height value. Roughness values were evaluated as root mean square roughness (Rq) and average roughness (Ra).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of PVA/S-PCDs composite films for food packaging
ce:section.ce:para:
To verify the efficacy of the prepared films for active packaging applications, pork meat and sliced apples were packaged with PVA/S-PCDs2% composite film. First, PVA/S-PCDs2% composite film was cut into 7 cm × 7 cm, and each side of the surface was treated with ultraviolet rays for 15 min. In the case of sliced apples, color change and browning index were investigated when stored at room temperature for 48 h. The color of sliced apples was measured in triplicate using a Chroma meter (CR-400, Konica Minolta, Tokyo, Japan). Color differences were recorded on the CIE L*a*b* scale, where L, a, and b-values represent the lightness, redness, and yellowness, respectively. Additionally, the total color difference (ΔE) was calculated using the following formula:
Δ E =

where ΔL, Δa, and Δb are the difference between each color value of the film sample and the standard plate (L = 97.75, a = −0.49, and b = 1.96), respectively.

The browning index (BI) of sliced apples was calculated using the measured L*, a*, and b* values as following formula (Zielińska & Pankiewicz, 2020):
BI =

In the case of pork, changes in the color index, pH, and bacterial growth were evaluated every 2 days while stored at 4 °C for 8 days. Bacterial growth in packaged samples was measured using the total viable colony count method (Khan, Riahi, Kim, & Rhim, 2024). For this, 5 g of unpacked pork was homogenized in 45 mL of 0.1% peptone water for 3 min using a stomacher (BagMixer 400, Interscience, France). After serial dilution, homogeneous aliquots (100 μL) were spread on nutrient agar plates and incubated at 37 °C for 48 h. Results were expressed as Log CFU/g per colony.

All measurements were performed at least in triplicate (n ≥ 3) using freshly prepared samples and reported as means and standard deviations (mean ± SD). Statistics for the completely randomized design were performed with one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) using SPSS software (SPSS Inc., Chicago, IL, USA). Statistical differences between means were detected using Duncan's multiple range test (p < 0.05).

The photographs of P. aeruginosa, PCDs, and S-PCDs under visible light and UV light are shown in Fig. 2 a and b. P. aeruginosa is well known to produce fluorochromes known as pyocyanin and pyoverdine (Díaz-Pérez et al., 2023), which emit blue light under UV excitation at 365 nm. The fluorescent properties of live P. aeruginosa, a type of pathogenic bacteria known to cause infections in humans, have the potential to be utilized as raw materials for carbon dots synthesis and converted into valuable active packaging materials. The yellow color of the PCDs and S-PCDs solutions turned blue upon UV excitation at 365 nm because of the quantum confinement effect, confirming the successful synthesis of CDs. P. aeruginosa, PCDs, and S-PCDs showed an absorption peak at 290 nm (Fig. 2c) due to the transition of n-π* electrons from surface states to isolated electron pairs of functional groups (Zhou et al., 2017). Moreover, S-PCDs showed an additional peak at about 400 nm attributed to sulfur, confirming that it was successfully doped with sulfur (Cañas, Fronczek, Wagner, Latz, & Friedrich, 2014). The particle size of S-PCDs was recorded by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), and the result is illustrated in Fig. S1. The S-PCDs were monodispersed in spherical shape with less than 10 nm diameter (in the quantum range).

Photoluminescence (PL) intensities of P. aeruginosa, PCDs, and S-PCDs are compared in Fig. 2d. Synthesis of CDs increased the PL intensity of P. aeruginosa approximately 8 times (from 156.8 to 1264.0 a.u). However, the PL intensity of CDs decreased slightly after sulfur doping. Long, Wang, Cai, Rao, and Zhang (2020) reported the quenching effect of sulfur doping, where S doping of g-C3N4 photocatalysts reduced the PL intensity.

FT-IR spectra of P. aeruginosa, PCDs, and S-PCDs are shown in Fig. 2e. According to the FT-IR spectra of P. aeruginosa, the stretching vibration of O–H (3403 cm−1), N–H vibration (2358 cm−1) of ammonium ions, CC stretching (1636 cm−1) of amide Ⅰ, PO2 asymmetric stretching (1241 cm−1), PO stretching of phosphodiester, phosphorylated proteins, or polyphosphate product (1097 cm−1), and symmetric stretching vibration of phosphoryl groups (876 cm−1) were observed (Durve & Chandra, 2014; Ojeda & Dittrich, 2012). For PCDs and S-PCDs, the peaks located at 1668 cm−1 and 1387 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibration of CO and CH3, and CH2 asymmetric and symmetric deformation of proteins, respectively (Ighalo & Adeniyi, 2020). The absorption band around 1283 cm−1 may indicate SO3 stretching (Kang, Lee, & Kim, 2007). The absorption peak at 1032 cm−1 is attributed to stretching of C–O/C–C and bending vibrations of aromatic C–H. In addition, the characteristic band at 950 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of SO due to the presence of sulfur (Yang et al., 2020).

The PL spectra of PCDs and S-PCDs at excitation wavelengths ranging from 280 to 450 nm are shown in Fig. 3 . PCDs and S-PCDs showed a strong and broad emission peak in the blue region, and the emission was variable depending on the excitation wavelength (Li et al., 2018). The PL intensity of PCDs and S-PCDs gradually increased when the excitation wavelength was changed from 280 to 360 nm. Additionally, increasing the excitation wavelength (360–450 nm) reveals a clear and gradual bathochromic (red) shift of the emission peak towards lower peak intensity in both PCDs and S-PCDs. The highest PL intensities of both PCDs and S-PCDs appeared at around 440 nm with an excitation of 360 nm, indicating that both samples exhibit maximum absorption at this excitation wavelength, leading to the highest emission intensity. This result is consistent with the typical PL emission spectrum of CDs (Jafarifar et al., 2022), which can be influenced by various factors such as the energy level structure and surface states of the CDs (Liu, Xu, Niu, Gooding, & Liu, 2016).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibacterial and antioxidant activities of PCDs and S-PCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The antibacterial property of PCDs and S-PCDs against L. monocytogenes was shown in Fig. 4 . An inhibition zone of 21.63 ± 0.57 mm was observed with sulfur doping, whereas PCD did not exhibit antibacterial activity. S-PCDs exhibit strong antibacterial activity due to the presence of functional groups such as sulfonates and sulfonic acids. These groups can interact with the –SH functional group of enzymes to generate reactive oxygen species (ROS) that can cause damage to bacterial proteins and lipids, ultimately leading to bacterial death (Travlou et al., 2018). Moreover, it is suggested that sulfur metabolites can interact with cellular lipids to reduce total lipid content and inhibit bacterial survival through the release of cellular components.

The ABTS and DPPH radical scavenging activities of PCDs ranged from 28.13% ± 0.44% to 94.17% ± 0.00% and 17.44% ± 3.87% to 70.03% ± 0.97%, respectively (Table 1 ). By sulfur doping, the ABTS and DPPH radical scavenging activities increased from 34.60% ± 1.92% to 94.89% ± 0.56% and 28.31% ± 2.88% to 85.07% ± 2.18%, respectively. ABTS and DPPH radical scavenging rates were quantified as IC50, which is determined as the sample volume with 50% reduction in ABTS and DPPH. The IC50 values of PCDs and S-PCDs for ABTS were 170.58 μg/mL and 158.23 μg/mL, while the IC50 values of PCDs and S-PCDs for DPPH were 279.56 μg/mL and 215.78 μg/mL, respectively. The antioxidant power of a compound is classified according to its IC50 value; if the IC50 is less than 10 μg/mL, it is a very strong antioxidant; if the IC50 is in the range of 10–50 μg/mL, it is a strong antioxidant; if the IC50 is in the range of 50–100 μg/mL, it is a moderate antioxidant; if the IC50 is over than 100 μg/mL, it is a week antioxidant (Martiningsih, Mudianta, & Suryanti, 2021). Therefore, based on the previous studies on IC50 values, the proposed PCDs and S-PCDs may suggested that they can be considered as week antioxidants. Previous studies on microorganism-based CDs have consistently reported weak antioxidant activity. Kousheh, Moradi, Tajik, and Molaei (2020) reported that lactic acid-based CDs showed an IC50 value of approximately 1000 μg/mL, indicating weaker antioxidant activity compared to the PCDs and S-PCDs. In another study, S. cerevisiae–based CDs showed a high IC50 value of 570 μg/mL, indicating low antioxidant potential (Ghorbani, Tajik, Moradi, Molaei, & Alizadeh, 2022). Additionally, as reported by Sirin and Aslim (2020), CDs synthesized from lactic acid bacteria showed IC50 values exceeding 1000 μg/mL. Thererfore, compared to previously published microorganism-based CDs, the radical scavenging activity of PCDs and S-PCDs were validated as efficient antioxidant for bothe ABTS and DPPH assays.

The better antioxidant activity of CDs was obtained by the ABTS method compared to the DPPH assay because of the hydrophilic nature of CDs, resulting in superior dispersion of CDs in aqueous solution (Ezati & Rhim, 2021). The weak antioxidant activity observed in the DPPH assay is thought to originate from reduced interactions between CDs and DPPH radicals in methanol solution. The antioxidant activity of CDs originates from surface functional groups that can scavenge and dissipate ABTS and DPPH radicals by transferring H• from –OH to ABTS-H and DPPH-H on the surface of CDs.

In vitro cytotoxicity test was performed using an MTT assay on the L929 mouse fibroblast cell line to study the biocompatibility of the S-PCDs (Fig. S2). The fibroblasts' growth and proliferation were not adversely impacted by the presence of S-PCD, even after prolonged exposure of 72 h. Mean cell viability exceeding 90% was observed at all S-PCD concentrations for 24 h, and there were no significant differences compared to the control group. Furthermore, low cytotoxicity with cell viability exceeding >85% by the end of the testing period confirmed sufficient biocompatibility of S-PCD. Otis et al. (2019) also studied the cytotoxicity of Pseudomonas-based CDs through cytometry analysis utilizing HeLa and HEK293T cells. They reported there is no apparent effect of the PCDs on cell viability, even at a high concentration of PCDs. The outcomes of the biocompatibility test of the S-PCDs suggest their safety for food packaging and biomedical applications.

The visual appearance and SEM micrographs of the neat PVA, PVA/S-PCDs0.5%, PVA/S-PCDs1%, and PVA/S-PCDs2% are shown in Fig. 5 . The transparent color of neat PVA was changed to yellow by adding S-PCDs due to the brown-yellow color of S-PCDs (Fig. 5a). The neat PVA film showed a clean surface without any voids or cracks as shown in Fig. 5b. Small lumps were observed on the surface of the PVA/S-PCDs composite films, resulting in a significantly rougher appearance compared to the neat PVA film. However, S-PCDs were evenly distributed throughout the PVA polymer matrix, indicating the compatibility of CDs and PVA matrices (Min et al., 2023).

The elemental composition of the PVA/S-PCDs2% film surface was evaluated by EDS technique (Fig. 5c). Carbon (C), oxygen (O) and sulfur (S) elements were uniformly distributed in the film matrix. Since carbon and oxygen are constitutional components of PVA and PCDs, the sulfur dopped on the surface of PCDs is responsible for the additional elemental peak of the S component. The peak table of the EDX survey verified the weight percentage of the S element as 2.15%, and the percentage of C and O as 51.65% and 46.19%, respectively. Elemental mapping of PVA/S-PCDs2% film showed that the S element was evenly distributed on the film surface, further confirming that S-PCDs was evenly dispersed in the film matrix.

Fig. 5d and e shows the surface roughness of the neat-PVA film and PVA/S-PCDs composite films by two-dimensional (2D) and three-dimensional (3D) topographic AFM micrographs. AFM results showed that the surface roughness (Rq) of PVA-based films increased from 14.85 nm to 19.14 nm for PVA/S-PCDs1%. But, further increase of S-PCDs (2%) decreased Rq to 13.45 nm. The increase in Rq values can be assigned due to the good compatibility between PVA matrix and S-PCDs. However, the decrease in Rq value at high concentration of S-PCDs may be due to the hydrophilic nature of S-PCDs, further increasing the miscibility of added filler in the water soluble PVA matrix and ensuring the homogenous dispersion of S-PCDs in PVA film matrix. The increase in surface homogeneity indicates homogenous dispersion of S-PCDs and good compatibility between S-PCDs and PVA, similar to the previous study (Riahi, Priyadarshi, Rhim, & Bagheri, 2021). The excellent compatibility between PVA and S-PCDs may be due to the occurrence of oxygen functional groups on the S-PCDs surface, which form strong hydrogen bond interactions with the surface functional groups of the PVA backbone (Eskalen, Çeşme, Kerli, & Özğan, 2021).

Transparency and UV-barrier properties of the composite films were evaluated by measuring surface color and light transmittance (Table 2 ). The addition of S-PCDs significantly (p < 0.05) decreased the lightness (L-value) and redness (a-value) of the neat PVA, and significantly increased the yellowness (b-value) due to the brownish-yellow color of S-PCDs. The total color difference (ΔE) of the composite films also increased significantly (p < 0.05) by increasing S-PCDs concentrations. Moreover, the neat PVA film was highly transparent to UV light and visible light. The light transmittance of the neat PVA film at 280 nm was 77.45% ± 0.64% and significantly (p < 0.05) decreased to 43.16% ± 0.75% and 2.83% ± 0.58% for PVA/S-PCDs0.5% and PVA/S-PCDs2%, respectively. The results suggest that the UV-blocking properties of the prepared films can inhibit photooxidation of food and maintain its quality. The UV protective effect of composite films may be due to the UV absorbing ability of CDs (Das, Bandyopadhyay, & Pramanik, 2018).

The mechanical properties, water vapor permeability (WVP), and water contact angle (WCA) of the PVA-based composite films are shown in Table 3 . The tensile strength and Young's modulus of the neat PVA film were 25.67 ± 2.67 MPa and 2.29 ± 0.26 GPa, respectively. There was no significant difference (p > 0.05) in the tensile strength and Young's modulus according to the addition of various concentrations of CDs. However, the elongation of neat PVA film was 331.74% ± 14.61%, and increased to 354.04% ± 7.36%, 352.99% ± 17.03%, and 350.80% ± 19.99%, by addition of 0.5%, 1%, and 2% of S-PCDs, respectively. S-PCDs can act as a lubricant during the stretching process, which increases the elongation rate (Hu, Gu, Hu, Deng, & Wang, 2016). You, Zhang, Liu, and Lei (2016) reported a similar result that the addition of citric acid-derived CDs significantly increased the elongation of carboxymethyl cellulose-based films.

The addition of S-PCDs significantly (p < 0.05) increased the water vapor permeability (WVP) of the PVA film, while WCA (water contact angle) was significantly (p < 0.05) decreased. The enhancement of WVP can be attributed to the formation of convolutional pathways for water vapor diffusion created by the presence of hydrophilic S-PCDs in the PVA matrix (Ezati, Roy, & Rhim, 2022). Moreover, WCA was gradually decreased by adding more S-PCDs due to the hydrophilic nature of S-PCDs. The obtained WCA and WVP results are in good agreement with each other. It can be clearly seen that the WCA value of the film decreases as the concentration of S-PCDs increase, indicating the improved hydrophilicity of the composite films. In a similar way, WVP values increase at higher S-PCDs loading concentrations because increasing hydrophilicity allows more water molecules to evaporate over time.

As shown in Table 3, the aw value of the PVA-based composite films reached approximately 0.648 and it is significantly (p < 0.05) decreased to 0.610 by incorporation of S-PCDs. It is common knowledge that most bacterial growth is promoted at high aw of 0.990–0.995, although some foodborne bacteria, such as Staphylococcus aureus, can grow at a relatively low aw of 0.860 (Rodríguez, Sibaja, Espitia, & Otoni, 2020). Therefore, PVA/S-PCDs composite films are microbiologically stable aginst most foodborne bacteria, including E. coli and L. monocytogenes.

Fig. 6 showed antioxidant activities of the PVA-based composite films evaluated by the ABTS and DPPH assays. The ABTS and DPPH radical scavenging activities of neat PVA were 5.51% ± 1.20% and 4.87% ± 1.55%, respectively. The antioxidant activity of the neat PVA film was mostly attributed to the hydroxyl groups of PVA (Tamer et al., 2022). The ABTS and DPPH radical scavenging activities were significantly (p < 0.05) increased from 90.06% ± 1.70%–100% ± 0.00% and from 46.74% ± 1.70%–61.54% ± 0.76%, respectively, after addition of S-PCDs. CDs are known for their ability to neutralize free radicals and exhibit antioxidant properties, which are achieved through an electron transport mechanism in which CD molecules exchange electrons with free radicals. In addition, antioxidant activity by CDs has been reported to be due to numerous electron donating groups such as hydroxyl and carboxyl groups (Kousheh et al., 2020). This study showed that antioxidant activity was more prominent in the ABTS assay compared to the DPPH assay. The water-soluble nature of the PVA matrix can promote the release of S-PCDs in the hydrophilic ABTS solution. In contrast, the release of S-PCDs from the PVA matrix and their interaction with DPPH radicals is limited because the low solubility of PVA in hydrophobic DPPH solution results in low antioxidant activity in the DPPH assay. Antioxidant packaging materials such as PVA/S-PCDs composite films can be used to prevent food spoilage due to oxidation during storage. This type of active packaging is particularly effective in inhibiting food from discoloration due to oxidation or in inhibiting high-fat foods from rancidity.

The release of S-PCDs from PVA-based films was determined using food simulant solutions (Fig. S3). After immersing the PVA-based films in water, 50% ethanol, and 4% acetic acid solution, the S-PCDs were promptly released within the first 1 h, followed by a gradual decrease in the release rate until reaching a stable state after 2 h. The release rate of S-PCDs was affected by the concentration of S-PCDs, and as the concentration of S-PCDs increased, the release rate of S-PCDs also increased under all simulants. The maximum quantity of released S-PCDs was 36 μg/mL for the PVA/S-PCDs2% film that was employed in the real packaging experiment. This value was determined using a film measuring 2.5 cm × 2.5 cm. The maximum potential release of S-PCDs from a 7 cm × 7 cm film that is used in real packaging tests will be estimated to be 282.2 μg/mL, even in a liquid medium. However, based on the cytotoxicity investigation, the threshold of S-PCDs was 500 μg/mL, as indicated in Fig. S2. Therefore, it is noteworthy that the release of S-PCDs from all developed films remained below the cytotoxicity safety level of S-PCDs. This observation implies that the composite film designed for active packaging can be considered a safe material for the intended study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of PVA/S-PCDs composite films for food packaging

ce:section.ce:para: The effect of PVA/S-PCDs composite film on the quality of fresh minced pork was investigated to show the potential of active packaging materials. Fig. 7 a and b shows changes in the visual appearance of minced pork after 8 days of storage. The surface color of packaged minced pork turned red and darkened over time during storage under sunlight due to the conversion of myoglobin to brown met-myoglobin by UV-catalyzed photooxidation (Moradi, Tajik, Mardani, & Ezati, 2019). The change in a-value representing the degree of redness of minced pork was measured using a colorimeter, and the results are shown in Fig. 7c. The a-value of minced pork wrapped with PVA/S-PCDs2% composite film was significantly (p < 0.05) lower than that of meat wrapped with neat PVA film, indicating the UV blocking and antioxidant effect of the PVA/S-PCDs2%. Additionally, bacteria growth and pH changes in meat products during storage can lead to spoilage and reduced shelf life. The total viable colony (TVC) and pH of minced pork are shown in Fig. 7d and e, respectively. The TVC of minced porks packaged with PVA/S-PCDs2% composite film was significantly (p < 0.05) lower than that of the meat packaged with neat PVA film after 8 days of storage. The TVC of minced porks packaged with neat PVA film increased from 4.79 to 7.61 Log CFU/mL, while the TVC of meat packaged with PVA/S-PCDs2% composite film increased to 6.95 Log CFU/mL, indicating the antibacterial efficiency of the PVA/S-PCDs2% composite film. The pH of minced porks packaged with PVA/S-PCDs2% composite film was significantly (p < 0.05) lower than the meat packaged with neat PVA film during storage. The pH of minced porks packaged with neat PVA film increased from 5.25 to 5.93, while the pH of meat packaging with PVA/S-PCDs2% composite film increased to 5.68. The pH level of meat is an important quality indicator that is closely linked to several other meat quality attributes such as tenderness, color, and water retention ability (Andrés-Bello, Barreto-Palacios, García-Segovia, Mir-Bel, & Martínez-Monzó, 2013). The pH level of meat can increase during storage due to protein degradation and the formation of ammonia and amines, which are normally associated with meat spoilage (Karabagias, Badeka, & Kontominas, 2011). The pH level of meat often rises as an indicator of spoilage when microbial count increase. Therefore, the pH results were consistent with the microbial results.

Fig. 8 shows the visual appearance, color parameters, and browning index (BI) of the sliced apple packaged with neat PVA film and PVA/S-PCDs2% composite film for 48 h storage at 25 °C. As a result, the fresh-cut apples wrapped with neat PVA film gradually became darker and more wrinkles were formed after 8 h of storage. On the other hand, the sliced apples wrapped with PVA/S-PCDs2% composite film maintained their color well. The a-value and b-value of sliced samples wrapped with neat PVA film increased significantly (p < 0.05) compared to the apple wrapped with PVA/S-PCDs2% composite film, and the brightness (L-value) of the sliced apples was significantly (p < 0.05) decreased in the control group. As a result, the browning index of the apple samples increased significantly (p < 0.05) with increasing a and b-values. The strong antioxidant activity of PVA/S-PCDs2% composite film as shown in Fig. 6 is an important factor in preventing the discoloration of fresh-sliced apples. The main cause of discoloration in fresh-cut apples is polyphenol oxidase-mediated browning (Zhang et al., 2021). Antioxidants are recognized as effective polyphenol oxidase inhibitors that can interact with food and prevent browning. The addition of S-PCDs to the PVA film is very effective in inhibiting the discoloration of sliced apples and maintains their high appearance quality.

Sulfur-doped Pseudomonas aeruginosa-derived carbon quantum dots (S-PCDs) were successfully synthesized through a hydrothermal method. S-PCDs showed high antioxidant activity and moderate antibacterial activity against L. monocytogenes. S-PCDs imparts UV-blocking property and antioxidant activity to PVA-based films. FTIR and SEM analyzes show that S-PCDs are compatible with the PVA polymer matrix. Incorporation of S-PCDs into the PVA film increased the elongation without sacrificing tensile strength and Young's modulus. The PVA/S-PCDs composite films showed moderate antimicrobial and excellent antioxidant activities, and was confirmed to inhibit microbial growth in minced pork and prevent browning in fresh-cut apples. Therefore, the PVA/S-PCDs composite film has the potential to be used as an environmentally friendly active packaging film, especially for food prone to oxidation.

Su Jung Hong: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Zohreh Riahi: Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Gye Hwa Shin: Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Jun Tae Kim: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.
